title: Simple latch hook rug
subtitle: A small geometric rug worked in pre-cut wool bundles on canvas
excerpt: Latch hooking uses a tool with a hinged latch to knot short pre-cut lengths of wool through a canvas grid. Each knot is tied around a canvas thread and locks into place when the latch closes. The canvas grid provides a natural pixel grid for geometric or pictorial designs.
sourceNotes: Latch hook rug making on canvas is a well-established craft with a long history of kit-based and DIY production. The knot method is consistent across latch hook instruction. Observation-led.

content.text: Latch hooking creates a knotted pile rug by tying pre-cut wool lengths onto a

locks around a horizontal canvas thread and cannot pull out once the density of surrounding knots builds up. The canvas grid allows designs to be plotted like pixels: each canvas square equals one knot. This project makes a 20 x 20 cm geometric square in two or three colours.

items.name: Rug canvas, 5 holes per 2.5 cm, 30 x 30 cm (10 cm larger than the finished rug)

items.qty: approx. 200 pieces for 20 x 20 cm at 2 knots per cm
items.name: Pre-cut rug wool bundles, main colour (7-8 cm cut lengths)
Pre-cut rug wool bundles, contrast colour(s)
Non-slip rug backing fabric or latex rug backing (optional)

content.text: Step 1: Mark the design on the canvas

Use a permanent marker to outline your geometric design on the canvas, colouring each zone with the corresponding colour. A simple geometric: a border of one colour, a diagonal cross of a second colour, and the remaining squares in the main colour. Work in rows from the bottom of the canvas upward (or top to bottom: either works, choose one and stay consistent).
Step 2: Tie the latch hook knot

Fold a wool piece in half over the shaft of the latch hook, holding both ends against the handle. Push the hook through a canvas hole from the front, under a horizontal canvas thread, and back up through the hole above that thread. The latch should be open as the hook goes through. Loop both free ends of the wool over the hook tip. Pull the hook back toward you: as it pulls back, the latch closes around the wool ends and draws them through the fold, forming the knot. Tug both wool ends to tighten.
Tie one knot per canvas square, following the colour design. Complete one full row before moving to the next. Keep the work flat on a table and let the pile face upward as you work. The
is determined by the cut length of the wool: 7-8 cm pieces give a standard 3-4 cm pile.

When all knots are tied, turn the canvas over. Fold the 5 cm canvas border under to the back and stitch or glue it down. Apply a thin coat of latex rug backing to the back of the canvas and allow to dry fully before use: this locks the knots permanently and prevents the rug from slipping on the floor.

attrs.intro: Two problems specific to latch hooking.

items.fix: Re-check the knotting action: the latch must be fully open when going into the canvas and fully closed when pulling back. Slow down on each knot until the action is reliable. Apply latex backing to the back of the finished rug to lock all knots permanently.
items.cause: The latch did not close properly during knotting, so the ends were not pulled through the fold.
items.symptom: Wool pieces pull out when the rug is used

items.fix: Use a card template to cut all wool pieces to the same length. After completing a section, trim the pile lightly with scissors to even it out.
items.cause: The wool pieces were different lengths, or some were pulled through further than others during knotting.
items.symptom: Pile is uneven: some tufts much taller than others

attrs.heading: Common problems
